summary: Gruber argues that Palm has done nearly everything right since the iPhone's 2007 debut, betting the company on the Pre and WebOS built from scratch in two years. However, he questions whether even Palm's best effort is too little, too late given Apple's two-year head start in marketing, developer ecosystem, and global distribution. He contends the Pre's hardware keyboard appeals mainly to existing hardware keyboard users—a niche—and that Palm is better positioned to steal BlackBerry customers than iPhone customers. He also praises Palm's Mojo SDK as genuinely eating their own dogfood, unlike Apple's pre-App Store 'just write web apps' stance, while noting the Pre's multitasking approach reveals exactly why Apple restricts it on iPhone.
key_points: Palm recognized it was screwed after the iPhone launch and took drastic, company-betting action by hiring top talent and building WebOS from scratch in two years
Even if Palm matched the iPhone technically, Apple's two-year marketing lead, pop culture status, and virtuous developer ecosystem circle create a nearly insurmountable advantage
Hardware keyboards are a significant selling point only for existing hardware keyboard users—a niche—making the Pre a stronger BlackBerry competitor than iPhone competitor
The Pre's multitasking support, while praised by reviewers, inadvertently demonstrates exactly why Apple withholds it: users must police themselves or suffer performance degradation
Palm's Mojo SDK is fundamentally different from Apple's pre-App Store 'write web apps' period because Palm uses Mojo for its own apps, giving third-party developers equal footing
money_quotes: The iPhone was introduced at Macworld Expo on 9 January 2007. On that day, Palm Inc. was screwed.
They recognized that they were screwed and took drastic action. It's an overused phrase, but in this case it is true: they've bet the company.
It is hard to imagine how Palm could have produced anything better in the same amount of time. The question is whether even the best they could do is still too little, too late.
The iPhone has already joined the iPod as not just a tech culture hit, but a pop culture hit.
It is my theory that a hardware keyboard is a significant selling point for only one group of customers: those who already own a phone with a hardware keyboard, and that group is a niche.
The Pre is the BlackBerry Bold done right.

These exact words also summarize perfectly why Apple has withheld multitasking from third-party iPhone applications. The Pre will let you run too many apps at the same time and suffer the consequences. The iPhone will not.
Third-party WebOS apps have the potential to be just as nice as Palm's WebOS apps, because they're all written using the Mojo APIs.
key_insight: Palm may have executed brilliantly on the Pre, but Apple's two-year ecosystem head start in marketing, distribution, and developer momentum means even a technically excellent competitor faces a nearly insurmountable gap—and the Pre's real opportunity lies in displacing BlackBerry, not iPhone.
